steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence reads: "no birder has ever achieved the ultimate [blank] an in-person glimpse of all 10,000-plus species of birds." After "ultimate goal," the gerund phrase "catching an in-person glimpse..." explains what the goal is. A colon can introduce a noun or gerund phrase that elaborates on a completed independent clause.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'goal catching': no punctuation between "goal" and "catching" creates a run-on — the reader cannot tell where the goal ends and the elaboration begins. ✗
  • Choice B — 'goal. Catching': a period makes "Catching an in-person glimpse of all 10,000-plus species" a sentence fragment with no main verb. ✗
  • Choice C — 'goal: catching': a colon after the independent clause ("no birder has ever achieved the ultimate goal") correctly introduces the gerund phrase that defines the goal. ✓
  • Choice D — 'goal; catching': a semicolon must join two independent clauses. "Catching an in-person glimpse..." is not an independent clause, so a semicolon is incorrect here. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
